topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the coffee shop felt suddenly thin, like the oxygen had been deliberately removed. I watched Liam laugh, a bright, easy sound that always used to resonate with me. Now, it just bounced off the wall of my chest, hollow. He was talking animatedly about some shared memory, a moment I vividly recalled—a ridiculous summer adventure—and he just nodded, a polite, distant acknowledgment. When he turned back to me, his eyes swept over me, registering nothing more than a familiar face in the periphery. A tight knot formed low in my stomach, a cold, hard pressure. I picked up my lukewarm latte, the ceramic suddenly feeling heavy and insignificant in my hand. I didn't say anything. I just took a slow, deliberate sip, letting the silence stretch until it felt taut enough to snap.

[story 2]

"So, that trip to the coast," Liam was saying, gesturing expansively with his hands, his voice warm and nostalgic. I leaned against the counter, trying to project an air of casualness, but my jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I listened, my gaze fixed on the way his eyes sparkled when he recalled the event. Then, he paused, searching for something, and his expression shifted—a flicker of confusion, maybe even mild disorientation. He looked right *through* me, as if I were a piece of furniture in the background. A strange, bitter taste rose in my throat. I pushed off the counter, my movements stiff. "Right," I managed, the word clipped and devoid of warmth. I didn't wait for a reply, turning sharply toward the exit, the sound of my own footsteps echoing too loudly in the sudden quiet of my mind.

[story 3]

I stood by the lockers, watching Liam and Sarah share a private joke. It was the same easy camaraderie we used to share, the effortless synchronization of two people who had built a world together. When Liam finally glanced my way, it wasn't recognition that crossed his features; it was the blankness of someone encountering a stranger. A slow, internal tightening began in my shoulders, pulling my collar up around my neck. I felt a sudden, intense need to examine myself, to find the flaw that had made me invisible to him. I crossed my arms, planting my feet firmly on the linoleum floor. My expression settled into one of cool detachment, a deliberate shield against the unexpected chill that had settled deep within my core. I let the silence hang between us, heavy and accusatory.
